longTailKeywords: how long does acute bronchitis last
_meta.authoringNotes: Numbers cross-verified against 2006 ACCP cough guideline, CDC Be Antibiotics Aware program, 2017 Smith AAFP review, Cochrane Review 2017 (Smith) on antibiotics for acute bronchitis, NICE NG120 (2019 cough), and Ebell 2013 median cough duration data.

h1Title: Acute Bronchitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute bronchitis is a self-limited inflammation of the large airways that produces a cough lasting one to three weeks, usually following an upper respiratory tract infection. Roughly 5% of US adults develop it each year, making it one of the top ten reasons for primary care visits. The cough is the defining feature — productive in about half of cases, dry in the rest, often persisting long after fever and congestion resolve. Viral infection accounts for more than 90% of cases; bacterial causes are rare. Despite this, an estimated 70-80% of patients still receive antibiotics, which provide minimal benefit and drive antimicrobial resistance. Symptomatic care, reassurance, and patience are the cornerstones of treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: ~5% of US adults annually (CDC NAMCS); 10 million outpatient visits/year
keyStats.demographics: Slightly higher incidence in winter months, smokers, and people exposed to secondhand smoke or air pollution
keyStats.avgAge: Affects all ages; peak presentation in adults 20-50
keyStats.globalCases: Among the top five reasons for primary care visits worldwide

definition: Acute bronchitis (ICD-10: J20.9) is an acute, self-limited inflammation of the trachea and large bronchi caused most often by a respiratory virus. The hallmark is a cough that lasts longer than five days and resolves within one to three weeks, usually preceded or accompanied by symptoms of an upper respiratory tract infection such as sore throat, runny nose, low-grade fever, and headache. Pathologically, viral infection of the bronchial epithelium triggers shedding of ciliated cells, neutrophilic inflammation, and airway hyperreactivity that can persist for weeks after the virus has cleared. Mucus production may be clear, yellow, or green — sputum color reflects neutrophil activity and is not diagnostic of bacterial infection. By definition, the diagnosis applies to people without underlying chronic lung disease; the same illness in a patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is classified as an acute COPD exacerbation. The condition is managed predominantly in primary care, with pulmonology referral reserved for cough that persists beyond eight weeks or for patients with atypical features suggesting an alternative diagnosis.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for more than 90% of cases. Common pathogens include influenza A and B, respiratory syncytial virus, parainfluenza, rhinovirus, adenovirus, and seasonal coronaviruses. SARS-CoV-2 now produces a clinically indistinguishable picture.

Less than 10% of cases. Confirmed pathogens include Bordetella pertussis (rising again in adults), Mycoplasma pneumoniae, and Chlamydophila pneumoniae. Standard pyogenic organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae) are not established causes in immunocompetent adults.

A distinct entity in pediatric pulmonology defined by a wet cough lasting more than four weeks that resolves with two weeks of amoxicillin-clavulanate. Recognized as a separate diagnosis from adult acute bronchitis.

primarySymptoms: Cough lasting longer than five days and typically one to three weeks, which is the cardinal feature — the median duration is 18 days (Ebell 2013).
Sputum production in roughly 50% of patients, which may be clear, yellow, or green; color does not reliably distinguish viral from bacterial infection.
Preceding or accompanying upper respiratory symptoms such as runny nose, sore throat, mild headache, and nasal congestion in the first three to five days.
Low-grade fever below 38.5°C in some patients; a fever above 38°C that persists beyond four days raises suspicion for pneumonia.
Chest discomfort or burning behind the breastbone, particularly during coughing fits.
Wheezing or mild shortness of breath, especially on exertion, reflecting transient airway hyperreactivity that can persist for weeks after the infection clears.
Fatigue and malaise that often outlast the cough by several days, with patients reporting reduced exercise tolerance for two to three weeks.
Hoarseness from associated laryngeal involvement, common when influenza or parainfluenza is the underlying pathogen.
Mild post-tussive vomiting in severe coughing fits, classically associated with Bordetella pertussis in adults.
earlyWarningSigns: A sore throat and runny nose progressing to a cough lasting more than five days
New wheezing or chest tightness in a previously healthy person during a viral illness
Cough disturbing sleep for more than three nights in a row
Sputum that becomes streaked with blood during forceful coughing (small amounts are common; persistent or large-volume hemoptysis is not)
emergencySigns: Shortness of breath at rest, inability to speak in full sentences, or oxygen saturation below 92% on a home pulse oximeter — evaluate urgently for pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, or asthma exacerbation
Fever above 38.5°C lasting more than four days, rigors, or pleuritic chest pain — community-acquired pneumonia must be excluded
Persistent coughing of blood beyond a few streaks, or large-volume hemoptysis — requires same-day imaging and pulmonology review
Cough lasting more than eight weeks — reclassified as chronic cough and warrants formal workup for asthma, GERD, postnasal drip, ACE-inhibitor effect, or pertussis

causes.description: More than 90% of acute bronchitis is viral. Influenza A and B, respiratory syncytial virus, parainfluenza, rhinovirus, adenovirus, seasonal coronaviruses, and SARS-CoV-2 all produce the same clinical picture. The virus damages ciliated epithelium, exposing nerve endings and producing the persistent cough reflex.

Responsible for an estimated 10-20% of adult cough illness lasting more than two weeks in immunized populations. Waning vaccine immunity in adolescents and adults has produced a resurgence in many countries. The cough is often paroxysmal, with post-tussive vomiting and a prolonged convalescent phase.

Together account for under 10% of acute bronchitis. Both produce a dry, persistent cough that can blur the line between bronchitis and atypical pneumonia. More common in school-age children, college students, and military recruits.

Even after the virus is cleared, damaged bronchial epithelium remains hyperreactive for two to eight weeks. This is why coughing often outlasts other infectious symptoms and why bronchodilators help patients with audible wheeze.

Active smoking, secondhand smoke exposure, wood smoke, occupational dust, and high-particulate air pollution all sensitize the bronchial mucosa and prolong recovery. Smokers have longer cough duration and higher recurrence rates than non-smokers.
Subtle aspiration of nasopharyngeal contents during a viral upper respiratory infection seeds the lower airways and amplifies the inflammatory response, particularly during sleep.

riskFactors.description: Smokers have a 60-70% higher annual incidence of acute bronchitis, more severe symptoms, and longer recovery. Smoking also raises the risk of progression to chronic bronchitis.
Adults living with smokers and children in smoking households have measurably higher rates of acute bronchitis and prolonged cough.
PM2.5 above 35 µg/m³ and occupational exposures (mining, construction, agriculture, hairdressing chemicals) increase bronchitis incidence and lengthen recovery.
Younger immune systems and age-related declines in cough strength and mucociliary clearance raise risk and complication rates at the extremes of age.
Around 80-90% of acute bronchitis cases follow a viral cold or flu, with cough beginning a few days into the illness as the infection descends from the nasopharynx into the bronchi.
HIV infection, chemotherapy, post-transplant immunosuppression, and chronic steroid use raise both incidence and the probability of bacterial co-infection.

riskFactors.factor: Unvaccinated status for influenza, pertussis, and COVID-19
riskFactors.description: Annual influenza vaccination reduces bronchitis incidence by roughly 20-40% in seasons with a good vaccine match. Tdap booster every 10 years reduces pertussis-related cough illness in adults.
Pre-existing airway inflammation predisposes to longer and more severe post-viral cough; allergic rhinitis is associated with a roughly two-fold increase in physician-diagnosed acute bronchitis.
Daycare, schools, dormitories, military barracks, and shared transport raise viral transmission rates and explain seasonal clustering.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 10 million US outpatient visits per year list acute bronchitis as the primary diagnosis (CDC NAMCS).
Incidence peaks in late autumn and winter, mirroring influenza, RSV, and seasonal coronavirus circulation.
Smokers have roughly twice the lifetime episode rate of never-smokers and longer mean cough duration.
Pertussis-associated bronchitis is rising in adolescents and adults due to waning vaccine immunity, with reported case rates several-fold higher than three decades ago in many high-income countries.
diagnosisOverview: Acute bronchitis is diagnosed clinically. The defining features are an acute cough lasting more than five days, normal vital signs (or a brief low-grade fever), normal lung sounds or scattered wheezes and coarse breath sounds, and the absence of focal findings such as crackles, dullness to percussion, egophony, or oxygen saturation below 95%. The role of testing is to exclude pneumonia and other dangerous mimics rather than to confirm bronchitis. A chest radiograph is recommended only when vital signs are abnormal (heart rate >100, respiratory rate >24, temperature >38°C, or oxygen saturation <95%), when focal examination findings are present, when the patient is elderly or immunocompromised, or when symptoms have not improved after three weeks. Procalcitonin testing has been studied as a tool to reduce inappropriate antibiotic prescribing: a low procalcitonin (<0.25 ng/mL) supports withholding antibiotics and has been shown in randomized trials to cut antibiotic exposure by roughly 30-40% without harming outcomes. Routine sputum cultures, viral PCR panels, and CRP testing are not required for typical cases. Influenza and COVID-19 rapid testing are appropriate during seasonal circulation because the results change treatment (oseltamivir, nirmatrelvir-ritonavir) and isolation guidance. The most consequential differential is community-acquired pneumonia; the most commonly missed alternative diagnosis in adults with a prolonged cough is pertussis, which deserves targeted testing in patients with paroxysmal cough, post-tussive vomiting, or recent contact with a known case.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The diagnostic mainstay. Acute cough longer than five days, no fever above 38°C beyond the first few days, normal heart and respiratory rate, oxygen saturation ≥95%, and absence of focal crackles on auscultation supports the diagnosis and effectively rules out pneumonia in low-risk adults.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician asks about symptom onset, sputum, fever, prior episodes, smoking and exposure history, and vaccination status. They listen to your lungs and check your pulse, breathing rate, temperature, and oxygen saturation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used to exclude pneumonia, not to confirm bronchitis. Indicated when vital signs are abnormal, focal lung findings are present, the patient is over 75 or immunocompromised, or symptoms have not improved after three weeks.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard two-view radiograph that takes a few minutes. There is no preparation; the radiation dose is roughly equivalent to ten days of natural background exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A reading at or above 95% on room air is strong evidence against significant lower respiratory tract involvement. Saturations below 92% warrant urgent evaluation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small clip is placed on a fingertip for under a minute. The test is painless and immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Helps guide whether antibiotics are needed. Levels below 0.25 ng/mL support withholding antibiotics; trial data show procalcitonin-guided prescribing reduces antibiotic exposure substantially with no rise in adverse outcomes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Many labs return results within hours; some primary care clinics offer point-of-care assays.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used during seasonal circulation because positive results enable specific antiviral therapy (oseltamivir within 48 hours of onset, nirmatrelvir-ritonavir for high-risk COVID-19 patients) and inform isolation guidance.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nasal or nasopharyngeal swab. Antigen results come back within 15-30 minutes; PCR results typically the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Indicated when cough lasts longer than two weeks and is paroxysmal, accompanied by post-tussive vomiting or an inspiratory whoop, or when there has been contact with a known case. Public health notification is required in most jurisdictions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: PCR uses a nasopharyngeal swab and is most useful within the first three weeks of cough. Serology is used later in the illness.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Considered in outbreak settings, prolonged cough in young adults or military recruits, or atypical features. Routine testing is not recommended for sporadic mild cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nasopharyngeal or oropharyngeal swab; results in 24-72 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of acute bronchitis is symptomatic in the great majority of cases. The CDC Be Antibiotics Aware program, the American College of Chest Physicians cough guideline, and NICE NG120 all agree: antibiotics should not be prescribed routinely. Cochrane review evidence shows antibiotics shorten cough duration by roughly half a day at the cost of measurable harms (rash, gastrointestinal upset, Clostridioides difficile infection, and population-level resistance). Despite this, an estimated 70-80% of US patients with acute bronchitis still receive antibiotics, making this condition the single largest target for outpatient antibiotic stewardship. First-line management centers on rest, hydration, and education that the cough is expected to last two to three weeks. Pharmacologic options that target symptoms include dextromethorphan or codeine-containing antitussives for dry cough disrupting sleep, guaifenesin for thick sputum, and inhaled short-acting beta-agonists such as albuterol when audible wheeze or evidence of bronchospasm is present. Honey (one to two teaspoons before bed, age over one year) outperforms placebo for nocturnal cough in trials and is a reasonable adjunct. Oral or inhaled corticosteroids are not routinely indicated for otherwise healthy adults; oseltamivir is appropriate for influenza-positive patients within 48 hours of symptom onset, and nirmatrelvir-ritonavir is indicated for high-risk adults with confirmed COVID-19 within five days. Antibiotics are reserved for three specific scenarios: confirmed or strongly suspected pertussis (azithromycin 500 mg on day one then 250 mg daily for four days, primarily to reduce transmission rather than shorten the patient's own illness), confirmed Mycoplasma or Chlamydophila pneumoniae (doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for seven days, or a macrolide), and patients with significant comorbid disease such as severe COPD, advanced heart failure, or immunocompromise where the threshold for empiric coverage is lower. Smokers should be offered counseling and pharmacotherapy for tobacco cessation at every visit — it is the single intervention with the largest long-term respiratory benefit.

medicalTreatments.name: Symptomatic care (rest, hydration, humidified air, throat lozenges)
medicalTreatments.description: The foundation of acute bronchitis management. Patients should be reassured that the cough is expected to persist for two to three weeks and given specific return-precautions for worsening dyspnea, high fever, or hemoptysis. Adequate fluid intake (1.5-2 L/day in healthy adults) thins secretions; humidified air eases throat and airway irritation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Manages symptoms in over 90% of immunocompetent adults; outcomes are not improved by antibiotics.

medicalTreatments.name: Dextromethorphan (10-20 mg every 4 hours as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: An over-the-counter antitussive that suppresses the cough reflex centrally. Most useful for dry, sleep-disrupting nocturnal cough. Avoid combining with serotonergic medications; not recommended for productive cough where mucus clearance is preferred.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest reduction in cough severity scores; best evidence for short-term relief of dry cough.

medicalTreatments.name: Guaifenesin (200-400 mg every 4 hours, max 2.4 g/day)
medicalTreatments.description: An expectorant that increases respiratory tract fluid and helps thin sputum, easing mucus clearance. Available over the counter as a single agent or combined with antitussives or decongestants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Patient-reported ease of expectoration improves modestly; objective effect on cough duration is small.

medicalTreatments.name: Short-acting beta-agonists (albuterol metered-dose inhaler 90 mcg, 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended only for patients with audible wheeze, chest tightness, or evidence of bronchospasm. They do not benefit patients without wheeze, and Cochrane data do not support routine use in all comers with acute bronchitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cough and breathlessness in 60-70% of patients with documented wheeze; no clear benefit in those without.

medicalTreatments.name: Honey (1-2 teaspoons, ages 1 and older)
medicalTreatments.description: Inexpensive, safe, and supported by trial evidence for reducing nocturnal cough frequency and severity in children and adults. Not for infants under 12 months due to botulism risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Superior to placebo and comparable to dextromethorphan in head-to-head pediatric trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Oseltamivir (75 mg twice daily for 5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for laboratory-confirmed or strongly suspected influenza, ideally started within 48 hours of symptom onset. Most useful for patients at higher risk of complications (over 65, pregnant, immunocompromised, or with significant cardiopulmonary disease).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Shortens influenza symptom duration by roughly one day when started within 48 hours; benefit smaller after that window.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (500 mg day 1, then 250 mg daily for 4 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used specifically for confirmed or strongly suspected pertussis. The primary goal is to reduce transmission to vulnerable contacts (infants, pregnant women) rather than to shorten the patient's cough, which is usually well-established before treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces nasopharyngeal carriage and transmission; minimal effect on cough duration after the first three weeks of illness.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (100 mg twice daily for 7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for confirmed Mycoplasma or Chlamydophila pneumoniae bronchitis. A macrolide such as azithromycin is an alternative. Treatment is not indicated empirically for typical viral bronchitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Shortens cough duration and symptom burden in confirmed atypical bacterial infection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Honey for nocturnal cough
alternativeTreatments.description: Multiple randomized trials and a Cochrane review (Oduwole 2018) support honey as effective in reducing nocturnal cough frequency and severity in children over one year and adults. Safe, inexpensive, and widely available.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pelargonium sidoides extract (EPs 7630)
alternativeTreatments.description: A South African geranium root extract with consistent randomized-trial evidence (Matthys 2010) for reducing bronchitis symptom severity and shortening cough duration by roughly two days versus placebo. Available over the counter in much of Europe as Umckaloabo.

whySeeSpecialist: Most acute bronchitis is managed in primary care and does not require pulmonology referral. Specialist evaluation is warranted when cough persists beyond eight weeks, when there is recurrent acute bronchitis (more than three episodes per year), when there is unexplained hemoptysis or weight loss, when imaging shows abnormalities that do not resolve, or when the patient has underlying chronic lung disease that complicates the picture.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a primary care physician who follows CDC and ACCP cough guidance — specifically, one who is willing to explain why antibiotics are usually not helpful rather than reflexively prescribing them. For persistent cough beyond eight weeks, seek a pulmonologist with experience in chronic cough workup and access to spirometry, bronchoprovocation testing, and laryngoscopy.
preventionStrategies: Receive an annual influenza vaccination — reduces bronchitis incidence by 20-40% in well-matched seasons and prevents hospitalization in high-risk groups
Stay up to date with COVID-19 boosters and Tdap pertussis boosters every 10 years to reduce respiratory infection incidence and severity
Stop smoking and avoid secondhand smoke — the single largest modifiable risk factor for both incidence and prolonged recovery
Practice hand hygiene, especially during respiratory virus season, and avoid touching the face after contact with shared surfaces
Wear a well-fitted mask in crowded indoor settings when a household member or close contact has an active respiratory illness
Improve indoor air quality with HEPA filtration and adequate ventilation during high-pollution or wildfire smoke days
lifestyleModifications: Rest fully for the first three to five days of illness rather than pushing through — fatigue worsens recovery time and prolongs viral shedding
Increase fluid intake to 1.5-2 L per day to keep secretions thin, unless restricted for other medical reasons
Use a cool-mist humidifier or sit in a steamy bathroom for 10-15 minutes when coughing fits are severe
Avoid known airway irritants — cigarette smoke, wood smoke, strong cleaning chemicals, cold dry air — during recovery
Sleep with the head of the bed slightly elevated to reduce postnasal drip and nighttime coughing
Resume exercise gradually after symptoms improve, starting with low-intensity walking and building back to baseline over one to two weeks

recommended: Warm broths and soups to soothe airway irritation and maintain hydration
Honey-sweetened warm tea or warm water with lemon (ages 1 and older)
Citrus fruits, berries, and vegetables rich in vitamin C and zinc
Adequate protein intake to support immune function during recovery
avoid: Alcohol — dehydrates and can worsen mucus thickening
Sugar-sweetened beverages, which provide no nutritional support during illness
Very cold drinks if they trigger coughing fits
Heavy fried foods that slow recovery and worsen gastrointestinal upset alongside antibiotics or NSAIDs

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid strenuous exercise during the acute illness, particularly while febrile, because exertion can worsen airway inflammation and prolong recovery. Once fever has resolved and energy returns, restart with light walking and build gradually over 7-14 days. Persistent wheezing or breathlessness on exertion beyond three weeks deserves clinical reassessment for asthma or post-infectious airway hyperreactivity.
dailyManagement: Track temperature, oxygen saturation, and severity of cough in a simple diary during the first two weeks
Keep a clear plan for when to seek care — fever above 38.5°C beyond four days, breathlessness at rest, oxygen saturation under 92%, or blood in sputum
Use prescribed inhalers correctly if wheeze is present — confirm spacer technique with a pharmacist or clinician
Avoid driving and operating machinery if taking sedating cough suppressants such as codeine
Wash hands frequently and avoid close contact with infants and immunocompromised people, particularly during the first week of illness
prognosis: Acute bronchitis is overwhelmingly self-limited in immunocompetent adults. Roughly half of patients are cough-free by day 18, and 85-90% have fully resolved by week three. Fatigue and exercise intolerance can persist for an additional one to two weeks. Serious complications are rare in otherwise healthy people: progression to pneumonia occurs in well under 5% of cases, and hospitalization is uncommon. The risk of complications is concentrated in older adults, smokers, and patients with chronic cardiopulmonary disease or immunocompromise. Recurrent acute bronchitis — more than three episodes per year — should prompt evaluation for undiagnosed asthma, post-nasal drip syndrome, gastroesophageal reflux, immunodeficiency, or environmental exposures. Long-term, repeated episodes in active smokers raise the risk of progression to chronic bronchitis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients feel meaningfully better within 7-10 days; the median cough duration is 18 days, with 85-90% of patients fully recovered by three weeks. Wheeze and post-infectious airway hyperreactivity can take six to eight weeks to fully settle. A cough lasting longer than eight weeks is reclassified as chronic cough and requires a different diagnostic workup.
complications: Progression to community-acquired pneumonia, occurring in under 5% of immunocompetent adults; signaled by new fever, focal crackles, or oxygen saturation below 95%
Post-infectious airway hyperreactivity, with wheeze and exercise intolerance persisting for six to eight weeks; sometimes unmasks previously undiagnosed asthma
Acute exacerbation of underlying COPD or asthma when bronchitis develops in a patient with chronic lung disease
Transmission of pertussis to unvaccinated infants, who are at risk of severe and sometimes fatal disease — a major reason for prompt diagnosis and treatment of adult pertussis
Adverse effects of inappropriate antibiotic use, including rash, gastrointestinal upset, Clostridioides difficile colitis, and antibiotic resistance at the population level

supportResources.name: CDC — Acute Bronchitis (Chest Cold)
supportResources.description: US public-health patient education on chest cold, when antibiotics help, and when they do not.

supportResources.name: American Lung Association — Acute Bronchitis
supportResources.description: Patient education and symptom resources from the leading US lung health organization.

supportResources.name: NICE NG120 — Cough (acute): antimicrobial prescribing
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline on managing acute cough and when antibiotics are warranted; freely accessible.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia produces focal lung findings (crackles, dullness, egophony), a chest X-ray showing infiltrate, persistent high fever, raised respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation often below 95%. Acute bronchitis lacks these features and has a normal chest X-ray. When vital signs are abnormal or focal findings are present, imaging is mandatory to exclude pneumonia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma produces episodic wheeze, chest tightness, and cough triggered by allergens, exercise, or cold air, with reversibility on spirometry and a history of recurrent episodes. Acute bronchitis is a single self-limited illness following a viral upper respiratory infection. New wheeze after a viral illness can unmask previously undiagnosed asthma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD is a chronic, irreversible airflow limitation usually from long-term smoking, with daily symptoms outside of exacerbations and a confirmatory spirometry result (FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator). The same viral illness in a COPD patient is classified as an acute COPD exacerbation, not acute bronchitis, and is treated differently.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis with Postnasal Drip
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic sinusitis produces facial pressure, nasal obstruction, anterior or posterior nasal discharge, and a cough that worsens when lying down rather than the diffuse cough of bronchitis. Cough persisting beyond eight weeks with prominent postnasal drip should prompt evaluation for upper airway cough syndrome rather than repeated bronchitis.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: The most consequential differential diagnosis; up to 5% of acute bronchitis cases progress to or are reclassified as pneumonia.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-bronchitis airway hyperreactivity can unmask previously undiagnosed asthma; recurrent episodes warrant spirometry.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Repeated episodes in smokers raise the risk of chronic bronchitis and progression to COPD.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Postnasal drip from sinusitis is a leading cause of cough lasting more than eight weeks and can mimic recurrent bronchitis.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: GERD is one of the three most common causes of chronic cough alongside asthma and upper airway cough syndrome; suspect it when bronchitis-like cough persists.

faqs.question: How long does acute bronchitis last?
faqs.answer: The cough lasts a median of 18 days, with 85-90% of patients fully recovered within three weeks. Fever and upper respiratory symptoms usually resolve in the first week. A cough lasting longer than eight weeks is reclassified as chronic cough and needs separate workup.

faqs.question: Do I need antibiotics for acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Almost never. Over 90% of cases are viral, and Cochrane data show antibiotics shorten cough by only half a day while causing harm. CDC, ACCP, and NICE all advise against routine use. Exceptions are confirmed pertussis, Mycoplasma, or significant comorbid disease.

faqs.question: How can I tell if it is acute bronchitis or pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Pneumonia is more likely with fever above 38°C beyond a few days, heart rate over 100, respiratory rate above 24, oxygen saturation under 95%, focal crackles, or pleuritic chest pain. A chest X-ray showing an infiltrate confirms it. Acute bronchitis has normal vitals and a normal X-ray.

faqs.question: Is acute bronchitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes — the underlying viruses spread through respiratory droplets and contaminated surfaces. Transmission risk is highest in the first week. Hand hygiene, covering coughs, and staying home while febrile reduce spread. Pertussis is particularly dangerous to unvaccinated infants.

faqs.question: Does the color of my sputum tell me if I need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No. Yellow or green sputum reflects neutrophils responding to airway inflammation and occurs in both viral and bacterial infections. Studies show sputum color does not predict bacterial cause or antibiotic benefit and should not drive prescribing decisions.

faqs.question: What home remedies actually work for acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Evidence-supported measures include rest, fluids, a cool-mist humidifier, honey for nocturnal cough (ages 1+), and avoidance of smoke. Over-the-counter dextromethorphan helps dry cough; guaifenesin can ease mucus clearance. Most other folk remedies lack robust evidence.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for a cough?
faqs.answer: See a clinician for temperature above 38.5°C lasting more than four days, shortness of breath at rest, oxygen saturation under 92%, cough beyond three weeks without improvement, coughing of blood, chest pain on breathing, or any cough in an infant or immunocompromised person.

faqs.question: Can acute bronchitis turn into pneumonia?
faqs.answer: It can, but absolute risk is under 5% in immunocompetent adults. Risk rises in older adults, smokers, and those with chronic lung, heart, or immune disease. Warning signs include new fever, worsening breathlessness, focal chest pain, or oxygen saturation below 95%.

faqs.question: Why is my cough getting worse at night?
faqs.answer: Lying flat increases postnasal drip into the airways, the cough reflex sensitises during sleep, and airway dryness peaks overnight. Elevating the head of the bed, using a humidifier, taking honey before bed, and an evening dose of dextromethorphan all reduce nighttime cough.

faqs.question: Is acute bronchitis the same as a chest cold?
faqs.answer: Yes — chest cold is the everyday name for acute bronchitis. Both refer to viral inflammation of the large airways producing a cough lasting one to three weeks, usually following an upper respiratory infection. The CDC uses the term chest cold in patient-facing antibiotic stewardship materials.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Avoid strenuous exercise while febrile or in the first few days, since exertion can worsen airway inflammation and prolong recovery. Light walking is usually fine. Resume normal activity gradually once fever resolves, building back to baseline over one to two weeks.

faqs.question: Can children get acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Yes, though in children under five it is often bronchiolitis or a viral upper respiratory infection. A wet cough lasting more than four weeks can represent protracted bacterial bronchitis — a distinct entity that responds to a two-week course of amoxicillin-clavulanate.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute and chronic bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Acute bronchitis is a single self-limited episode lasting one to three weeks, almost always viral. Chronic bronchitis is productive cough on most days for at least three months in two consecutive years, almost always from long-term smoking, and is one phenotype of COPD.

faqs.question: Do bronchodilator inhalers help acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Only when there is wheeze or evidence of bronchospasm. Cochrane review data show no benefit from beta-agonist inhalers in patients without wheeze. For patients who do wheeze, a short course of albuterol can reduce cough and breathlessness while the airway hyperreactivity settles.

faqs.question: How much does acute bronchitis treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Symptomatic care is inexpensive — over-the-counter dextromethorphan, guaifenesin, and honey cost a few dollars. The largest cost is usually the primary care visit. Antibiotics, when indicated, are generic and low-cost. Avoiding unnecessary antibiotics saves money and avoids harm.

faqs.question: Can air pollution cause acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Pollution does not directly cause infection, but it raises bronchitis incidence and prolongs recovery by inflaming the airway lining. Wildfire smoke, occupational dust, and indoor wood-stove smoke are documented contributors. HEPA filtration, masks, and ventilation reduce risk.

faqs.question: Should I get a chest X-ray for my cough?
faqs.answer: Not routinely. A chest X-ray is recommended only when vital signs are abnormal, focal lung findings are present, symptoms have not improved after three weeks, or the patient is elderly, immunocompromised, or has significant cardiopulmonary disease.

faqs.question: Why does my cough hang on after I feel otherwise better?
faqs.answer: Viral infection damages ciliated airway cells and leaves the bronchi temporarily hyperreactive. After the virus clears, cold air, exercise, or strong smells can still trigger cough until the lining regenerates. This post-infectious cough can last six to eight weeks.

faqs.question: Can pertussis cause acute bronchitis in adults?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bordetella pertussis causes an estimated 10-20% of adult cough illness lasting longer than two weeks. Suspect it when cough is paroxysmal, accompanied by post-tussive vomiting or an inspiratory whoop, or after contact with a known case. Diagnosis is by nasopharyngeal PCR.

faqs.question: Does smoking make acute bronchitis worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Smokers have higher annual incidence, more severe symptoms, longer cough, and higher recurrence. Tobacco smoke also raises the risk that repeated episodes progress to chronic bronchitis and COPD. Smoking cessation slows this progression and shortens recovery.

metaDescription: Acute bronchitis: a viral chest cold causing cough for 1-3 weeks. Symptoms, diagnosis, when antibiotics help, and prognosis reviewed by our medical board.

images.altText: Medical illustration of inflamed bronchial airways showing mucus build-up characteristic of acute bronchitis
images.caption: Acute bronchitis — inflammation and mucus production in the large airways of the lungs.

images.altText: Diagram of the human respiratory system showing the trachea, primary bronchi, and bronchial tree affected by acute bronchitis
images.caption: Where acute bronchitis occurs — inflammation of the trachea and large bronchi.

images.altText: Normal posteroanterior chest X-ray with clear lung fields, used to exclude pneumonia in patients with suspected acute bronchitis
images.caption: A normal chest X-ray — used to exclude pneumonia rather than confirm acute bronchitis.
